title: B Cells in NASH/MASH: Zotero-Active Draft

references.title: The Emerging Role of B Cells in the Pathogenesis of NAFLD
Microbiota-Driven Activation of Intrahepatic B Cells Aggravates NASH Through Innate and Adaptive Signaling
Bacterial components-driven intrahepatic CXCR5hi B cells are important population for MASH progression through inducing inflammation
Single-cell transcriptomic landscape of intrahepatic B Cells in MASH

paragraphs.text: NASH, increasingly referred to within the newer MASH terminology, is not only a hepatocyte stress disorder but also an immune-remodeling state in which adaptive immune cells can shape inflammatory progression. Within that adaptive compartment, B cells have moved from being viewed as bystanders to being studied as candidate amplifiers and regulators of NAFLD/NASH pathogenesis

Experimental evidence places B cells directly in the gut-liver axis of NASH. Intrahepatic B cells can be activated in a microbiota-driven manner and aggravate NASH through innate and adaptive immune signaling

. More recent work further identifies intrahepatic CXCR5hi B cells induced by bacterial components as an inflammatory population that can express TNF-alpha, CD80, and MHC class II and promote T-cell activation in MASH models

For a grant introduction, the most defensible framing is therefore not simply that total B-cell abundance is increased, but that specific B-cell states and niches may matter. Single-cell profiling of intrahepatic B cells in MASH supports this subset-oriented view and provides a rationale to separate pathogenic, regulatory, and antigen-presenting B-cell programs in future studies

claims.claim: B cells are an emerging adaptive immune component in NAFLD/NASH pathogenesis rather than merely bystander lymphocytes.
claims.note: Review focused on B-cell roles in NAFLD/NASH pathogenesis.

claims.claim: Microbiota-driven activation of intrahepatic B cells can aggravate NASH through innate and adaptive immune signaling.
claims.note: Direct mechanistic Hepatology study; the title and metadata explicitly match the claim.

claims.claim: Bacterial components can induce an intrahepatic CXCR5hi B-cell population with inflammatory features in MASH.
claims.note: Abstract states CXCR5hi B cells are induced by bacterial components and express inflammatory/antigen-presenting markers.

claims.claim: Single-cell profiling supports a subset-oriented view of intrahepatic B-cell biology in MASH.
claims.note: Direct single-cell transcriptomic study of intrahepatic B cells in MASH.
